title: Case 95470: College Decision 065/26/COL - Norway - Request for authorisation for an exception from the rules on driving and resting due to exceptional circumstances (Charity truck run) - 2026
text:
Case No: 95470
Document No: 1590486
Decision No: 065/26/COL
EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY DECISION
of 8 April 2026
to grant Norway an exception from the application of Article 8(6) of Regulation (EC) No
561/2006 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 15 March 2006 on the
harmonisation of certain social legislation relating to road transport and amending Council
Regulations (EEC) No 3821/85 and (EC) No 2135/98 and repealing Council Regulation
(EEC) No 3820/85
THE EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY,
Having regard to the Act referred to at point 24e in Chapter II of Annex XIII to EEA
Agreement,
Regulation (EC) No 561/2006 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 15
March 2006 on the harmonisation of certain social legislation relating to road transport
and amending Council Regulations (EEC) No 3821/85 and (EC) No 2135/98 and
repealing Council Regulation (EEC) No 3820/85 (“the Act”),
as adapted to the EEA Agreement by Protocol 1 thereto, in particular Article 14(1) thereof,
Whereas:
1 Relevant EEA law and the request by the Norwegian Government
Article 1 of the Act reads, in extract:
“This Regulation lays down rules on driving times, breaks and rest periods for
drivers engaged in the carriage of goods and passengers by road in order to
harmonise the conditions of competition between modes of inland transport,
especially with regard to the road sector, and to improve working conditions and
road safety. […]”
Article 4(h) of the Act reads:
“‘weekly rest period’ means the weekly period during which a driver may freely
dispose of his time and covers a ‘regular weekly rest period’ and a ‘reduced weekly
rest period’:
— ‘regular weekly rest period’ means any period of rest of at least 45 hours,
— ‘reduced weekly rest period’ means any period of rest of less than 45 hours,
which may, subject to the conditions laid down in Article 8(6), be shortened to a
minimum of 24 consecutive hours;”
Article 8 of the Act reads, in extract:
“1. A driver shall take daily and weekly rest periods.
[…]
6. In any two consecutive weeks a driver shall take at least:
Avenue des Arts 19H, 1000 Brussels, tel: +32 2 286 18 11, www.eftasurv.int

Page 2
(a) two regular weekly rest periods; or
(b) one regular weekly rest period and one reduced weekly rest period of at least
24 hours.
A weekly rest period shall start no later than at the end of six 24-hour periods from
the end of the previous weekly rest period.
[…]
6b. Any reduction in weekly rest period shall be compensated by an equivalent
period of rest taken en bloc before the end of the third week following the week in
question.
[…]”
Article 14(1) of the Act reads:
“Provided that the objectives set out in Article 1 are not prejudiced, Member States
may, after authorisation by the Commission, grant exceptions from the application
of Articles 6 to 9 to transport operations carried out in exceptional circumstances.”
By letter dated 21 January 2026 (Doc No 1588417), the Norwegian Government sent a
request to the Authority asking for authorisation to grant an exception from the application
of Article 8(6) of the Act to a “charity truck run” that will be held on 13 June 2026.
In its application, the Norwegian Government specified that the request for an exception is
itself based on a request made by a private entity that wants to organise the
aforementioned charity truck run. The truck run will collect money and gifts for children who
are hospitalised with cancer, and the drivers will take children on a short truck ride. For the
event, the private entity has organised escort trucks, both from the police and three
approved convoy trucks. The truck run will lead to what the Norwegian Government calls a
“minor breach” in the weekly resting period of the truck drivers, limited to less than three
hours to approximately 10 drivers.
In its request, the Norwegian Government argues that such truck runs constitute “transport
operations carried out in exceptional circumstances” and, as such, under Article 14(1) of
the Act qualify for an exception from the rules on driving and resting time in Article 8(6) of
the Act.
2 The Authority’s assessment
In its request the Norwegian Government points to the charitable nature of the truck-run,
that the Authority has previously granted materially equivalent exemptions (Decisions No
24/25/COL, 038/19/COL, 028/18/COL and 095/17/COL and) and the fact that the exception
is minor with regard to the duration and the scope of the exception.
Furthermore, the Norwegian Government claims that an exception would not run counter
to the purposes of the Act as enshrined in Article 1 of the Act. In this regard, the Norwegian
Government cites two decisions taken by the European Commission, namely Decisions
C(2009) 10651(Netherlands) and C(2011) 39642(Belgium).
In Decision (C(2009) 1065), the Commission authorised the Netherlands, on the basis of
Article 14(1) of the Act, to grant an exemption from Article 8(6) of the Act to drivers taking
part in charitable truck-runs for handicapped children during periods of weekly rest. The
drivers were participating in the truck-runs once per year, and on a voluntary basis.
According to the decision, these drivers were exempted from the obligation to take
compensatory rest for the reduced weekly rest period. The Commission took into account,
in particular, the non-commercial nature of the truck runs and the fact that no competitive
1Available at: http://www.chauffeursverenigingen.nl/sites/default/files/truckruns/Beschikking.EG.pdf
(in Dutch only).
2Available at: https://www.digitach.be/Content/Documents/nl/besluit22062011.pdf (in Dutch) and
https://www.digitach.be/Content/Documents/fr/decision22062011.pdf (in French).

Page 3
advantage arose from carrying out the truck runs and that they did not have any negative
influence on road safety. The Commission concluded that, as a consequence, the truck
runs did not prejudice the objectives set out in Article 1 of the Act.
In Decision (C(2011) 3964), the Commission authorised Belgium, on the basis of Article
14(1) of the Act, to grant an exception from Article 8(6) of the Act to drivers taking part in
truck-runs that were organised once per year, on a voluntary basis. The truck-runs in
question were philanthropic and non-commercial in nature and consisted in transports of
handicapped children through towns and small villages in Belgium, at reduced speeds,
during weekends. The Commission underlined the fact that the truck-runs did not confer
any competitive advantage to the undertakings concerned, did not compromise the working
conditions of the drivers and had no negative influence on road safety. The Commission
also took note of the fact that the Belgian authorities put in place specific measures in order
to enable the competent authorities to control the truck-runs and to safeguard that there
was no abuse. In light of these circumstances, the Commission concluded that the
objectives set out in Article 1 of the Act were not prejudiced by the truck-runs and
authorised Belgium to exempt the drivers of the truck-runs from the application of Article
8(6) of the Act.
It appears that the truck-run described in the present request from the Norwegian
Government is similar to those that were the subject of the abovementioned decisions of
the Authority. Furthermore, the truck run is largely comparable to the truck-runs that were
the subject of the abovementioned decisions by the Commission. This truck-run as
described in the request will also be carried out at a weekend, for a charitable cause (i.e.
not of a commercial nature and therefore not conferring any competitive advantages) and
does not appear to compromise road safety.
The Authority notes that the legal framework has been amended since the above
mentioned decisions as well as Decisions No 038/19/COL, 028/18/COL and 095/17/COL.
Regulation (EU) 2020/1054 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 15 July 2020
amending Regulation (EC) No 561/2006 as regards minimum requirements on maximum
daily and weekly driving times, minimum breaks and daily and weekly rest periods and
Regulation (EU) No 165/2014 as regards positioning by means of tachographs was
incorporated into the EEA Agreement by Decision 78/2022 of 18 March 2022 and entered
into force on 1 April 2024. Additionally since Decision 24/25/COL was issued the Act has
been amended by Regulation (EU) 2024/1258 of the European Parliament and of the
Council of 24 April 2024 amending Regulation (EC) No 561/2006 as regards minimum
requirements on minimum breaks and daily and weekly rest periods in the occasional
passenger transport sector and as regards Member States’ power to impose penalties for
infringements of Regulation (EU) No 165/2014 committed in another Member State or in a
third country which was incorporated the EEA Agreement by Decision 42/2026 of 6
February 2026 and entered into force on 7 February 2026. Although certain amendments
were made to Article 8 by that amending act, it does not have a material effect on the
outcome of the present request.
In conclusion, taking into account the decisions described above, the Authority takes the
view that the truck run, as described in the request, is a transport operation carried out in
exceptional circumstances, and authorises Norway, on the basis of Article 14(1) of the Act,
to grant an exception from the application of Article 8(6) of the Act.
HAS ADOPTED THIS DECISION:
1. Norway may grant an exception from the application of Article 8(6) of the Act
referred to at point 24e in Chapter II of Annex XIII to the EEA Agreement, Regulation
(EC) No 561/2006 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 15 March 2006
on the harmonisation of certain social legislation relating to road transport and

Page 4
amending Council Regulations (EEC) No 3821/85 and (EC) No 2135/98 and
repealing Council Regulation (EEC) No 3820/85, as adapted to the EEA Agreement
by Protocol 1 thereto, in conformity with Article 14(1) of the Act, to drivers
participating in a charity truck run on 24 May 2025.
2. The Decision is addressed to the Kingdom of Norway.
3. The Decision shall enter into force upon notification to the Kingdom of Norway.
4. This Decision shall be authentic in the English language.
Done at Brussels,
For the EFTA Surveillance Authority
Arne Røksund Árni Páll Árnason Nuscha Wieczorek
President Responsible College Member College Member
For Melpo-Menie Joséphidès
Countersigning as Director,
Legal and Executive Affairs
This document has been electronically authenticated by Arne Roeksund, Sigrun Ingibjorg
Gisladottir.
